Differences between procedural and objec: Procedural programming is a top-down approach focusing on functions and sequences of actions, often leading to global data access issues. Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a bottom-up approach that encapsulates data and functions into secure entities called 'objects'. OOP introduces powerful features like Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Data Hiding, making it ideal for complex, scalable software.
Reading and writing data using cin and c: In C++, standard input and output are handled by streams. `cin` is an object of the `istream` class used with the extraction operator `>>` to read data from the keyboard. `cout` is an object of the `ostream` class used with the insertion operator `<<` to print data to the console. They provide type-safe I/O compared to C's printf/scanf.
Features of Input/output Streams: C++ I/O streams are sequences of bytes. The `iostream` header provides `istream` (for input) and `ostream` (for output). Streams are automatically buffered, type-safe, and can be overloaded for user-defined classes, allowing seamless I/O operations for custom objects.
Creating classes: A class is a user-defined blueprint from which objects are created. It is declared using the `class` keyword followed by the class name. Inside, it contains data members (variables) and member functions (methods) that define the properties and behavior of the object.
Class objects: An object is an instance of a class. When a class is defined, no memory is allocated until an object is instantiated. Objects are declared just like basic variables: `ClassName objectName;`. Each object holds its own copy of the class's non-static data members.
Accessing class members: Class members (data and functions) are accessed using the dot operator (`.`) for normal objects, or the arrow operator (`->`) for pointers to objects. However, you can only access members that are declared as `public` from outside the class. `private` members are hidden and can only be accessed by the class's own member functions.
Differences between Structures, Unions,: Structures (`struct`) group different data types, with members being public by default. Unions (`union`) share the same memory location for all members, saving memory but allowing only one member to be active. Enumerations (`enum`) define named integral constants. Classes (`class`) are like structures but their members are private by default, heavily used for OOP.
Inline and Non-inline member functions: Inline functions are expanded in line when they are invoked, eliminating function call overhead. Functions defined inside a class definition are implicitly inline. Non-inline functions are declared inside the class but defined outside using the scope resolution operator `::`. For large functions, non-inline is preferred to prevent code bloat.
Static data members and static member fu: Static data members belong to the class rather than any object, meaning all objects share the same single copy of the variable. Static member functions can only access static data members and other static functions. They are called using the class name and scope resolution operator (`ClassName::functionName()`).
Functions with Default parameters/argume: C++ allows functions to have default arguments. If a caller omits an argument, the default value is used. Default arguments must be specified from right to left in the parameter list. This reduces the need for function overloading in simple cases.
Inline Functions: The `inline` keyword requests the compiler to replace the function call with the actual code of the function. This improves execution speed for small, frequently called functions but can increase the binary size (code bloat). The compiler may ignore the request if the function is too complex.
Manipulator Functions: Manipulators are special functions used with I/O streams to format data. Common manipulators include `endl` (inserts newline and flushes stream), `setw` (sets field width), and `setprecision` (sets decimal precision). They are included via the `<iomanip>` header.
Function overloading and Scope rules: Function overloading allows multiple functions to have the same name but different parameter lists (type, number, or order). The compiler determines which function to call based on the arguments passed (compile-time polymorphism). Scope rules dictate that overloaded functions must exist in the same scope to overload each other.
Friend of a class (friend function and f: A `friend` function or class is granted access to the private and protected members of another class. It is declared using the `friend` keyword inside the class granting access. Friend functions are not member functions, but they are useful for operations that need deep access to two different classes, like operator overloading.
Reference variables: A reference variable is an alias, or an alternative name, for an existing variable. It is declared using the `&` operator. Unlike pointers, references must be initialized when declared and cannot be reassigned to refer to another variable later. They are heavily used in passing arguments by reference.
Differences between Call by value, Call: Call by value passes a copy of the argument; modifications do not affect the original. Call by address passes a pointer to the variable, requiring dereferencing to modify the original. Call by reference passes an alias to the variable, allowing direct modification without pointer syntax, making the code cleaner and safer.
Recursion (Function, Member Function): Recursion occurs when a function calls itself to solve a smaller instance of the same problem. It requires a base condition to terminate. Both standard functions and class member functions can be recursive. It is elegant for problems like factorials and tree traversals but can lead to stack overflow if not handled properly.
Void pointer: A void pointer (`void *`) is a generic pointer that can hold the address of any data type. However, it cannot be dereferenced directly because the compiler doesn't know the type of the target data. It must be typecast to a specific pointer type before dereferencing.
Pointer arithmetic: Pointers can be incremented (`++`), decremented (`--`), added to, or subtracted from integers. Pointer arithmetic scales according to the size of the data type it points to. For example, incrementing an `int *` moves the address forward by 4 bytes (size of int).
Pointer to pointer: A pointer to a pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another pointer. It is declared using multiple asterisks (e.g., `int **ptr`). This is heavily used in dynamic 2D arrays and when passing a pointer to a function to modify the pointer itself.
Possible problems with the use of pointe: A dangling pointer points to memory that has been deallocated. A wild pointer is uninitialized and points to a random memory location. A null pointer points to `nullptr` (0), and dereferencing it causes a crash. Proper initialization and setting pointers to `nullptr` after deletion prevents these severe bugs.
Differences between pointer and referenc: Pointers store memory addresses, can be reassigned, can be null, and require dereferencing (`*`). References are constant aliases for an existing variable, cannot be null, must be initialized upon declaration, and share the exact same syntax as normal variables once established.
Classes containing pointers: When a class contains a pointer as a data member, special care must be taken. Dynamic memory is usually allocated in the constructor and deallocated in the destructor. Default shallow copying will cause double-free errors, so a custom copy constructor and assignment operator (Deep Copy) are strictly required (The Rule of Three).
Pointer to objects: You can create pointers that point to class objects: `ClassName *ptr = new ClassName();`. Instead of the dot operator (`.`), the arrow operator (`->`) is used to access the object's member functions and variables through the pointer.
this pointer: The `this` pointer is a hidden, implicit pointer passed to all non-static member functions. It points to the object that invoked the function. It is essential when local variable names shadow class member names, allowing you to explicitly refer to the member as `this->variable`.
Pointer to data member: C++ allows pointers to specifically point to class members rather than specific instances. Declared as `DataType ClassName::*ptr`, it represents an offset rather than an absolute address. It is accessed using the `.*` or `->*` operators along with an actual object instance.
Array declaration and processing of mult: Multidimensional arrays (like 2D matrices) are arrays of arrays. They are declared as `int arr[rows][cols];`. In a class, they can be statically sized or dynamically allocated using pointers to pointers. They are typically processed using nested loops.
Array of objects: You can create an array where each element is an object of a class, e.g., `Student students[50];`. This automatically calls the default constructor for each object. You can use loops to initialize or call member functions for each object in the array.
The Standard C++ string class-defining a: The `<string>` library provides a highly flexible string class, avoiding the pitfalls of C-style character arrays. Strings can be defined and initialized easily: `string name = "John";`. They handle memory dynamically, resizing automatically when characters are appended.
Member functions: The `string` class has numerous member functions like `length()`, `empty()`, `substr()` for extracting parts of the string, and `find()` for locating substrings. These methods make string manipulation vastly superior to manual char array operations.
Modifiers of string class: Modifiers alter the string. `append()` or `+=` adds to the end, `insert()` places characters at a specific index, `erase()` removes characters, and `replace()` swaps substrings. These modifiers handle all underlying memory reallocation safely.
Opening and closing of files: File handling requires the `<fstream>` header. `ifstream` is used for reading, `ofstream` for writing, and `fstream` for both. A file is opened using the `open()` method or constructor, and must be closed using the `close()` method to flush buffers and release system resources.
Modes of file: File modes define how a file is opened. Common modes include `ios::in` (read), `ios::out` (write, overwrites file), `ios::app` (append to end), `ios::trunc` (truncate existing), and `ios::binary` (open in binary mode). Multiple modes can be combined using the bitwise OR operator `|`.
File stream functions: File streams inherit from standard streams, meaning you can use `<<` and `>>`. Additionally, `getline()` reads entire lines, `eof()` checks for the end of the file, `get()` reads single characters, and `put()` writes single characters.
Reading/Writing of files: Text files are read using the extraction operator `>>` (which skips whitespace) or `getline()`. They are written using the insertion operator `<<`. For complex text parsing, data is extracted into appropriate primitive types directly from the file stream.
Sequential access and random access file: Sequential access reads data from beginning to end. Random access allows jumping to specific locations in the file. C++ provides `seekg()` and `seekp()` to move the internal read and write pointers, and `tellg()` and `tellp()` to get their current positions.
Binary file operations: Binary files store data exactly as it appears in memory, avoiding character translation overhead. They are opened with the `ios::binary` mode. Data is read and written using the `read()` and `write()` methods, passing the memory address cast to a `char*` and the byte size.
Classes and file operations: Objects of a class can be written directly to a binary file using `write((char*)&obj, sizeof(obj))`. However, this is extremely dangerous if the class contains pointers or dynamic memory (like `std::string`), as only the pointer address is saved, not the actual data.
Structures and file operation: Plain Old Data (POD) structures are perfectly suited for binary file operations. An entire structure containing fixed-size arrays and primitive types can be dumped to disk and read back instantly using binary `read` and `write` functions, serving as a simple database.
Manager functions (constructors and dest: Constructors and destructors are special manager functions. Constructors are invoked automatically when an object is created to initialize memory and state. Destructors are invoked when an object goes out of scope to release resources. They have no return types.
Default constructor: A constructor that takes no arguments is a default constructor. If no constructors are defined by the programmer, the compiler generates a default one. It is essential when creating arrays of objects, as the array instantiation requires calling the default constructor for every element.
Destructors: A destructor has the same name as the class preceded by a tilde (`~`). It cannot take arguments and cannot be overloaded. It is responsible for cleaning up resources, closing files, and freeing dynamically allocated memory (using `delete`) before the object is destroyed.
Constructor with default arguments: Like regular functions, constructors can have default arguments. This allows a single constructor to act as both a default constructor and a parameterized constructor depending on how many arguments are provided during object creation.
Parameterized constructor: A constructor that accepts one or more arguments is a parameterized constructor. It allows objects to be initialized with specific, user-provided values at the exact moment of creation, ensuring the object starts in a valid state.
Copy constructor: A copy constructor initializes a new object as a copy of an existing object of the same class (`ClassName(const ClassName &obj)`). It is invoked during pass-by-value, return-by-value, and direct assignment. If a class manages dynamic memory, a custom Deep Copy constructor is strictly mandatory.
Initializer lists: Initializer lists are used in constructors to initialize member variables before the constructor body executes. Syntax: `ClassName(int a) : memberA(a) {}`. It is the *only* way to initialize `const` members, reference members, and base class constructors.
Operator Overloading (unary operator, bi: C++ allows redefining the meaning of operators (like +, -, *, ==) for user-defined classes. Unary operators (like `++`) act on one object and take no arguments when implemented as members. Binary operators (like `+`) act on two objects and take one argument (the right-hand side).
Type conversions - basic type to class t: A basic type is converted to a class type using a constructor that takes a single basic type argument. A class type is converted to a basic type using an overloaded casting operator, defined as `operator type() const { return value; }` inside the class.
Inheritance Basics – derived class and b: Inheritance is the mechanism where a 'derived' class acquires the properties and behaviors of a 'base' class. It models the 'IS-A' relationship and promotes massive code reusability. The derived class inherits all non-private members of the base class.
Modes (private, protected, public inheri: The inheritance mode defines the access restrictions of inherited members. Public inheritance keeps public/protected members as public/protected. Protected inheritance turns public members into protected. Private inheritance (the default) turns all inherited members into private members of the derived class.
Types (simple, multi-level, multiple and: Simple: One base, one derived. Multi-level: A derives from B, B derives from C. Hierarchical: Multiple classes derive from a single base. Multiple: One class derives from multiple base classes simultaneously, a powerful but complex feature of C++.
Resolving ambiguities in inheritance: In multiple inheritance, if two base classes have a function with the same name, calling it from the derived class is ambiguous. This is resolved using the scope resolution operator to explicitly specify the base class: `object.BaseClass::functionName();`.
Virtual base class: The Diamond Problem occurs when two classes B and C inherit from A, and D inherits from both B and C. D ends up with two copies of A. Declaring A as a `virtual` base class for B and C ensures that D only inherits a single, shared instance of A.
Overriding member functions: Function Overriding occurs when a derived class provides a specific implementation for a function already defined in its base class. It has the exact same signature. When called on a derived object, the derived version completely shadows the base version.
Order of execution of constructors and d: When a derived object is created, the Base class constructor executes FIRST, followed by the Derived class constructor. When the object is destroyed, the order is strictly reversed: the Derived class destructor executes FIRST, followed by the Base destructor.
Aggregation and Composition: These model the 'HAS-A' relationship. Aggregation is a weak association where the contained object can exist independently of the container (e.g., Department has Teachers). Composition is a strong lifecycle binding where the contained object dies with the container (e.g., Car has an Engine).
Dynamic memory allocation using new and: Memory is allocated at runtime on the Heap using `new`. `int *ptr = new int;` or `new int[10];`. Unlike C's malloc, `new` automatically calls the constructor. Memory must be explicitly freed using `delete ptr;` or `delete[] arr;` which automatically calls destructors.
Memory leak and allocation failures: A memory leak occurs when dynamically allocated memory is never deleted, permanently losing heap space until the program terminates. Allocation failures occur when the heap is full, causing `new` to throw a `std::bad_alloc` exception.
Compile and run time polymorphism: Compile-time (static) polymorphism is achieved through function overloading and operator overloading, where the compiler knows the exact function to call. Run-time (dynamic) polymorphism is achieved through virtual functions and base-class pointers, where the function call is resolved during execution based on the actual object type.
Early binding and late binding: Early binding (static binding) resolves function calls at compile-time based on the pointer type. Late binding (dynamic binding) delays the resolution until runtime, looking up the Virtual Table (V-Table) to call the function corresponding to the actual object the pointer refers to.
Virtual functions: A virtual function is declared in a base class using the `virtual` keyword and redefined in a derived class. When accessed through a base class pointer/reference, the compiler uses late binding to ensure the derived class's overridden function is executed, enabling true polymorphism.
Pure virtual functions: A pure virtual function is declared by assigning `0` to it: `virtual void display() = 0;`. It has no body in the base class. It forces any derived class to provide an implementation, serving as a strict interface contract.
Abstract classes and concrete class: A class containing at least one pure virtual function is an Abstract Class. You cannot instantiate an abstract class. A Concrete Class is one that implements all inherited pure virtual functions, allowing objects to be created from it.
Dynamic constructors: A dynamic constructor allocates memory dynamically (`new`) for its data members right at the time of object creation. This allows objects to have a dynamic, resizable internal state, essential for custom strings or data structures.
Virtual destructors: If a base class pointer points to a derived object, calling `delete` on the base pointer only executes the base destructor, causing memory leaks for the derived object. Marking the base class destructor as `virtual` ensures the derived destructor executes first, followed by the base destructor.
Introduction to Self-Referential class: A self-referential class is one that contains a pointer to an object of the same class. This is the fundamental building block of dynamic data structures like Linked Lists, Trees, and Graphs, allowing objects to link to one another in chains or networks.
Basics of exception handling: Exception handling is a mechanism to handle runtime anomalies (like division by zero or out-of-memory) without crashing the program. It separates error-handling code from the main business logic, leading to cleaner and more robust applications.
Exception handling mechanism: The mechanism relies on three keywords: `try`, `throw`, and `catch`. Code that might fail is placed in a `try` block. If an error occurs, an exception is `throw`n. A `catch` block immediately following the try block intercepts the exception and handles the error gracefully.
Throwing mechanism: When an error condition is detected, the `throw` keyword is used to throw an object, variable, or literal (e.g., `throw runtime_error("Error");` or `throw 404;`). The program instantly stops current execution and unwinds the stack looking for a matching catch block.
Catching mechanism: A `catch` block catches thrown exceptions matching its specified type. You can have multiple catch blocks for a single try block to handle different data types (int, strings, custom classes). A catch-all block `catch(...)` catches any exception regardless of type.
Rethrowing an exception: If a catch block cannot completely handle an exception or wants to log it and pass it up the call stack, it can rethrow the exception using the `throw;` keyword with no arguments. The exception is then caught by the next enclosing try-catch block.
Function template and class template: Templates enable Generic Programming. A function template allows writing a single function that works with any data type (e.g., a generic `swap(T& a, T& b)`). A class template defines a class blueprint that can operate on any data type (e.g., `Stack<int>`, `Stack<string>`).
Class template with inheritance: Templates can be combined with inheritance. A class template can inherit from another class template, a regular class can inherit from a specific instantiation of a class template, and a class template can inherit from a non-template base class. This provides extreme flexibility for generic frameworks.
Introduction to STL- Containers, Algorit: The Standard Template Library (STL) is a powerful library of generic classes and functions. Containers (like vector, list, map) store data. Iterators are smart pointers used to navigate containers. Algorithms (like sort, find) process the data. They work seamlessly together.
Container - Vector and List: A `vector` is a dynamic array that grows automatically. It provides fast random access but slow insertions/deletions in the middle. A `list` is a doubly-linked list. It provides extremely fast insertions/deletions anywhere but does not support direct random access (no `list[5]`).
